lessons.intro:
The Port of Santos, located on the southeastern coast of Brazil in the state of São Paulo, is the largest port in Latin America and one of the busiest in the Southern Hemisphere. Officially inaugurated on February 2, 1892, it has been the gateway for Brazilian exports — from coffee in the 19th century to soybeans, sugar, and manufactured goods today. Every year, millions of tons of cargo pass through its 64 berths, connecting Brazil to more than 190 countries across every ocean.

The Port of Santos Museum, housed in a heritage building designed by Engineer Weinschenck in 1902, preserves and celebrates this extraordinary legacy. With over 2,000 artifacts across 23 exhibition sections, the museum tells the story of a port that helped shape a nation. Among its treasures are the Lavoura locomotive from 1889, original coffee scales from the 1890s, and the bow fragment of the Air Giorgis ship — one of the rarest items in the collection. The museum first opened its doors on September 1, 1989, and has since welcomed visitors from around the world.

In this first lesson, you will learn the essential language of hospitality — how to greet international visitors, introduce yourself as a guide, and make a lasting first impression. By the end, you will be ready to say "Welcome to the Port of Santos Museum" with confidence and warmth that sets the tone for an unforgettable visit.
lessons.title: Welcome to the Port of Santos
lessons.objective: Learn how to greet international visitors, introduce yourself as a guide, and use the verb 'to be' naturally — all within the Port of Santos Museum context.

vocab.en: we are glad to have you

lessons.situation: Three researchers from the University of Rotterdam have just arrived at the Port of Santos Museum for a scheduled visit. They are standing at the entrance, looking a bit disoriented after a long international flight. They do not speak Portuguese and are relying entirely on English communication. You need to approach them warmly, introduce yourself as their guide, confirm their appointment, hand them visitor badges, and make them feel immediately welcome in the museum's heritage building.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

The verb 'to be' has three present forms — am (I), is (he/she/it), are (you/we/they). Unlike all other English verbs, 'to be' does NOT use do/does for questions or negatives.

Structure

Affirmative: Subject + am/is/are + complement
Negative: Subject + am not / is not (isn't) / are not (aren't) + complement
Question: Am/Is/Are + subject + complement?

Port of Santos Examples

I am your guide at the Port of Santos Museum today.
The museum is located inside a 1902 heritage building designed by Engineer Weinschenck.
We are the hospitality team at the Santos Port Authority.
Are you here to see the Lavoura locomotive from 1889?

Watch Out!

Do you be from Rotterdam? → Are you from Rotterdam?
The museum have 23 sections. → The museum has 23 sections. (different verb!)
Welcome in the museum. → Welcome to the museum.

Pro Tip

Drill contractions early — 'I'm your guide,' 'We're happy to have you,' 'It's on the second floor' sound natural and warm in hospitality. First impressions start with a smile and a confident 'Good morning!'

lessons.intro:
The Santos Port Authority, known as APS (Autoridade Portuária de Santos), is the public institution responsible for managing one of the most strategically important ports on the planet. As a federal entity linked to the Brazilian Ministry of Ports and Airports, the APS oversees everything from maritime safety and environmental protection to the cultural heritage of the port complex. It is the organization that keeps the gears of Brazilian foreign trade turning smoothly, 24 hours a day, 365 days a year.

The numbers behind the APS are staggering. In 2024, the Port of Santos handled a record 179.8 million tonnes of cargo and processed 5.4 million TEUs in container traffic. The port stretches along 13 kilometers of quays with 64 berths, connecting Brazil to over 600 ports in 200 countries. The APS also manages the Port of Santos Museum, which houses more than 2,000 artifacts documenting the port's evolution since 1892. From the Lavoura locomotive to original shipping manifests, these treasures are preserved under the authority's care.

When journalists, investors, or curious visitors ask "Who runs this place?", you need a clear and confident answer. In this lesson, you will learn to present the APS like a seasoned spokesperson — using Simple Present tense to describe what the organization does, how it operates, and why it matters to Brazil and the world.
lessons.title: The APS: Who We Are
lessons.objective: Learn to present the Santos Port Authority clearly and confidently in English — so when a journalist or visitor asks 'Who runs this place?', you've got a great answer ready.
lessons.situation: A Financial Times journalist is visiting the museum to write a feature article about Brazilian port infrastructure. She is sitting in the lobby with a notebook and recorder, and she approaches you with the question: 'Can you tell me a bit about the organization that runs this place?' She wants clear, quotable facts about the APS — its mission, its scale, its achievements. You need to give a professional, confident explanation that could end up in an international newspaper.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

In the Simple Present, add -s or -es to the base verb for third-person singular (he/she/it). Spelling rules: add -es after ss, x, ch, sh, o; change consonant+y to -ies (carry → carries).

Structure

Affirmative: Subject + verb(-s/-es) — 'The APS manages the port.'
Negative: Subject + do/does not + base verb — 'The APS does not operate ships.'
Question: Do/Does + subject + base verb? — 'Does the port handle soybeans?'

Port of Santos Examples

The APS manages the largest port in Latin America.
The Port of Santos handles 27% of Brazil's foreign trade.
Our institution oversees maritime safety across 64 berths and 13 km of quays.
The museum does not charge an entrance fee for school groups.

Watch Out!

The APS manage the port. → The APS manages the port.
Does the port manages cargo? → Does the port manage cargo?
The authority don't operate ships. → The authority doesn't operate ships.

Pro Tip

When describing what an organization does, Simple Present sounds authoritative and factual. 'The APS manages…' beats 'The APS is managing…' every time. Use it to pitch the port with confidence.

lessons.intro:
First impressions are lasting impressions, and in the world of hospitality, a warm greeting can set the tone for an entire visit. At the Port of Santos Museum, international visitors arrive with curiosity and high expectations — whether they are seasoned researchers from European universities, curious tourists on a layover, or diplomatic delegations representing foreign governments. The way they are received in the first sixty seconds shapes their entire experience and their impression of the institution.

The Port of Santos Museum welcomes visitors from all backgrounds, and each requires a different level of formality. A casual tourist from California expects a friendly, relaxed welcome, while a Japanese professor with a scheduled visit appreciates formal courtesies. The museum, with its 2,000+ artifacts spanning 23 exhibition sections inside a heritage building from 1902, attracts everyone from schoolchildren to ambassadors. Learning to read the room and match your greeting accordingly is one of the most valuable skills a port guide can develop.

In this lesson, you will master formal and informal greetings for different visitor profiles using modal verbs "May" and "Can." By the end, you will know exactly when to say "May I be of assistance?" versus "Can I help you?" — and you will nail that all-important first impression every single time.
lessons.title: First Contact: Greeting International Visitors
lessons.objective: Master formal and informal greetings for different visitor profiles — from casual tourists to VIP delegates. You'll learn when to use 'May I help you?' vs. 'Can I help you?' and nail that first impression every time.
lessons.situation: Three visitors show up at the museum reception at the same time: an American tourist in shorts and sandals with no appointment who just wants to look around, a Japanese professor named Dr. Tanaka who has a confirmed 10 a.m. scheduled visit for research purposes, and a German diplomat from the consulate in São Paulo who is 30 minutes early for an official courtesy call. You need to greet each one appropriately, manage the different formality levels, and ensure nobody feels neglected or rushed.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Can expresses informal permission and ability. May expresses formal permission. Could sits between them in politeness. No 'to' after any modal verb.

Structure

Modal + base verb (NO 'to'): 'May I help you?' / 'Can I show you around?'
Negative: cannot (can't) / may not
Question: Modal + subject + base verb?

Port of Santos Examples

May I show you to the VIP lounge, Your Excellency?
Can I grab you a brochure about the museum's 2,000+ artifacts?
You may take photographs in all 23 exhibit sections.
Could I offer you a coffee while we prepare your tour of the Lavoura locomotive?

Watch Out!

May I to help you? → May I help you?
Can I helping you? → Can I help you?
'Can I help you?' to an ambassador → 'May I be of assistance?' (use May for VIPs)

Pro Tip

In hospitality, default to 'May' with dignitaries and delegates, and 'Can' with casual tourists. Pair 'May I' with a smile and you'll sound professional AND warm — the hospitality sweet spot.

lessons.intro:
The history of the Port of Santos stretches back to the earliest days of Portuguese colonization in Brazil. Long before the grand inauguration of February 2, 1892, small wooden wharves dotted the shoreline of the Santos estuary, serving local fishermen and colonial traders who shipped goods between the coast and the São Paulo highlands. For centuries, Santos was a modest coastal settlement, but the forces of history were about to transform it into the economic engine of an entire nation.

The catalyst was coffee. By the mid-19th century, the lush plantations of the São Paulo hinterland were producing more coffee than any other region on Earth, and every single bean had to pass through Santos on its way to European and American markets. The São Paulo Railway, completed in 1867, connected the highlands to the coast, and coffee exports exploded. The coffee barons grew fabulously wealthy, constructing the magnificent Coffee Exchange Palace where global coffee prices were determined until 1957. At its peak, coffee represented 84% of Brazil's total exports. The port grew from a colonial wharf into a 13-kilometer industrial waterfront.

Today, the Port of Santos Museum preserves this remarkable story through its collection of over 2,000 artifacts — from the Lavoura locomotive of 1889 to original customs stamps and shipping documents. In this lesson, you will learn to tell the port's history using the Simple Past tense, bringing dates, facts, and stories to life for every visitor who asks "So when did all this begin?"
lessons.title: The Port of Santos: A Brief History
lessons.grammar: Simple Past — Regular and irregular verbs
lessons.objective: Tell the story of the Port of Santos in English using the Simple Past. You'll learn to describe historical events with confidence and make dates, facts, and stories come alive for your visitors.
lessons.situation: A retired British sea captain named Captain Williams is visiting the museum with his wife. He has a deep personal interest in maritime history and has visited ports around the world. He stops in front of a historical photograph and asks you: 'So when did all this begin? What is the story of this port?' He expects a knowledgeable, engaging 2-minute history overview that matches his expertise level — not a simplified tourist version.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Regular verbs: add -ed (opened, inaugurated). Irregular verbs: memorize unique forms (was/were, built, grew, began, drove). Use did + base form for questions and negatives.

Structure

Affirmative: Subject + past verb — 'The port opened in 1892.'
Negative: Subject + did not (didn't) + base verb — 'The port didn't exist before 1892.'
Question: Did + subject + base verb? — 'Did coffee drive the expansion?'

Port of Santos Examples

The Port of Santos was officially inaugurated on February 2, 1892.
The São Paulo Railway connected the coffee plantations to Santos in 1867.
Coffee drove the port's expansion — it represented 84% of Brazil's exports.
Engineer Weinschenck designed the museum building in 1902.

Watch Out!

The port was inaugurate in 1892. → The port was inaugurated in 1892.
The city didn't grew quickly. → The city didn't grow quickly.
The port opens in 1892. → The port opened in 1892. (past event = past tense)

Pro Tip

Use time markers to polish your narration: 'In 1892…,' 'During the coffee boom…,' 'By the mid-20th century…' They make storytelling smooth and professional for visitors.

lessons.intro:
Navigating a museum can be overwhelming for visitors, especially in an unfamiliar country where signs may be in a different language. The Port of Santos Museum spans multiple floors and exhibition areas, each housing unique treasures from the port's 130-year history. A visitor might arrive at the entrance looking for the restroom, the coffee exhibit on the upper floor, or the gift shop near the exit — and without clear, confident directions in English, their experience can quickly become frustrating.

The museum building itself, designed by Engineer Weinschenck in 1902, has a distinctive layout that visitors need help navigating. The ground floor houses the reception area, the main exhibit hall, and the maritime gallery. The upper floor is home to the coffee heritage section and the famous Lavoura locomotive from 1889. With 23 exhibition sections containing over 2,000 artifacts, there is a lot of ground to cover. Clear spatial orientation — knowing how to say "straight ahead," "on your left," "at the end of the hallway" — transforms a confusing visit into a smooth, enjoyable experience.

In this lesson, you will master the language of giving directions using imperatives. By the end, you will be able to guide any visitor anywhere in the museum — and around the broader port area — without them ever feeling lost.
lessons.title: Navigating the Museum: Giving Directions
lessons.objective: Give clear, friendly directions inside the museum and around the port area using imperatives. By the end, you'll be able to guide anyone anywhere without them getting lost.
lessons.situation: A family of four tourists from Canada — two parents and two children aged 8 and 12 — approaches you at the museum entrance looking confused. The father asks how to get from the main entrance to the restroom first, then to the coffee exhibit on the upper floor, and finally to the gift shop near the exit. The mother is pushing a stroller and needs to know about elevator access. The children are already wandering off toward the exhibits.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Imperatives use the base verb without a subject. The implied subject is 'you.' For negatives: Don't + base verb. Adding 'please' softens the command into a polite request.

Structure

Affirmative: Base verb — 'Go straight.' / 'Turn left.'
Negative: Don't + base verb — 'Don't touch the exhibits.'
Polite: Please + base verb — 'Please follow me.'

Port of Santos Examples

Go straight down the hallway to reach the maritime gallery.
Take the elevator to see the Lavoura locomotive on the upper floor.
Don't miss the bow fragment of the Air Giorgis ship — it's our rarest artifact.
Please follow me to the coffee heritage section with original scales from the 1890s.

Watch Out!

You go straight. → Go straight. (no subject in imperatives)
To turn left at the desk. → Turn left at the desk. (no 'to' before verb)
Don't to touch. → Don't touch the display cases.

Pro Tip

Use hand gestures while giving directions — visitors follow even if their English isn't perfect. 'Please go straight' sounds much warmer than just 'Go straight' in a hospitality setting.

lessons.intro:
Every day, dozens of vessels transit the Port of Santos navigation channel — container ships stacked high with colorful boxes, massive tankers carrying crude oil, bulk carriers loaded with millions of tons of soybeans and sugar. The port's infrastructure is a marvel of engineering: ship-to-shore cranes that tower over the waterfront, tugboats that nudge 300-meter ships into position, and 64 berths stretching for 13 kilometers along the estuary. From the windows of the Port of Santos Museum, visitors can often see this living spectacle unfold in real time.

Understanding the technical vocabulary of ships, docks, and cargo is essential for any guide who wants to answer the detailed questions that maritime professionals and curious tourists frequently ask. The Port of Santos handled a record 179.8 million tonnes in 2024, including 5.4 million TEUs of containerized cargo. It connects Brazil to over 600 ports in 200 countries through a 25-kilometer navigation channel maintained at approximately 15 meters depth. These are not just statistics — they are the vocabulary of daily life at the world's busiest agricultural export port.

In this lesson, you will build your port vocabulary toolkit and learn to use "there is / there are" to describe the infrastructure around you. By the end, you will impress any maritime visitor with your knowledge of vessels, equipment, and port operations.
lessons.title: Key Port Vocabulary: Ships, Docks & Cargo
lessons.objective: Build your port vocabulary toolkit. Learn to describe ships, docks, and cargo types using 'there is/there are' — and impress maritime visitors with your technical knowledge.
lessons.situation: A group of 10 logistics students from Hamburg University is visiting the port as part of their European study abroad program. They are standing at the museum's observation window, pointing at ships in the channel. Their professor asks you: 'What kinds of ships come here? What is the difference between a pier and a wharf? How many berths does this port have?' They expect detailed, accurate technical answers with proper terminology.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

There is introduces singular or uncountable nouns. There are introduces plural nouns. This structure describes what exists in a place — essential for port descriptions.

Structure

Affirmative: There is/are + noun — 'There is a terminal.'
Negative: There is not (isn't) / There are not (aren't) — 'There aren't any cruise ships today.'
Question: Is there / Are there + noun? — 'Are there any tankers in the channel?'

Port of Santos Examples

There are 64 berths along the Port of Santos' 13 km of quays.
There is a navigation channel approximately 25 km long and 15 meters deep.
There are over 2,000 artifacts in the museum's 23 exhibition sections.
Is there a container terminal operated by Santos Brasil on the left bank?

Watch Out!

It has a terminal on the waterfront. → There is a terminal on the waterfront.
Have many cranes in the port. → There are many cranes in the port.
There is many ships. → There are many ships. (plural = are)

Pro Tip

Use 'there is/are' to paint vivid pictures for visitors: 'There are massive gantry cranes, there's a tugboat guiding a Panamax vessel, and containers stacked six high along the waterfront.'

lessons.intro:
Small talk is one of the most underrated skills in international hospitality. A brief, friendly conversation about the weather, a visitor's trip, or local food recommendations can create a personal connection that elevates a standard museum visit into a truly memorable experience. At the Port of Santos Museum, where visitors arrive from every corner of the globe — from Tokyo to Toronto, from Lagos to London — the ability to chat naturally in English is what transforms a guide from an information provider into a genuine host.

The city of Santos itself provides endless material for small talk. Visitors are often amazed to learn that Santos holds the Guinness World Record for the longest beachfront garden at 5,335 meters, that Pelé debuted for Santos FC at just 15 years old, or that the Coffee Exchange Palace once determined global coffee prices. The port's record year of 179.8 million tonnes in 2024 is a great conversation starter with business visitors, while the heritage tram along the waterfront and the panoramic view from Monte Serrat appeal to tourists seeking local flavor.

In this lesson, you will master the art of small talk using the Present Perfect tense for experiences. You will learn to ask about travel, share tips about Santos, and keep conversations flowing naturally — because great hospitality is not just about information, it is about connection.
lessons.title: Making Small Talk with Visitors
lessons.objective: Master the art of small talk. Learn to chat naturally with visitors about travel, experiences, and impressions using the Present Perfect — because great hospitality isn't just about information, it's about connection.

vocab.en: Have you been to Brazil before?

lessons.situation: During a break between exhibit rooms, you are chatting with two visitors — Dr. Sarah from Melbourne, Australia, who is a marine biologist, and Mr. Hayashi from Osaka, Japan, who is a retired shipping executive. They are enjoying the tour and the conversation has turned casual. Dr. Sarah mentions how warm Santos is compared to Melbourne, and Mr. Hayashi asks about local seafood restaurants. Keep the conversation flowing naturally while learning about their backgrounds.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Have/Has + past participle describes experiences and life events without specifying when. Use ever in questions and never for negatives. Since = point in time, For = duration.

Structure

Affirmative: Subject + have/has + past participle — 'I've worked here for 5 years.'
Question: Have/Has + subject + (ever) + past participle? — 'Have you visited Santos before?'
Negative: Subject + have/has + never + past participle — 'I've never seen a container ship up close.'

Port of Santos Examples

Have you visited any other ports in South America?
I've been a guide at the museum since 2020.
Have you tried Santos coffee? This city was once the coffee capital of the world.
The port has handled over 179.8 million tonnes in its record year of 2024.

Watch Out!

I have visited London yesterday. → I visited London yesterday. (specific time = past simple)
Have you went to the beach? → Have you been to the beach?
She has work here for years. → She has worked here for years.

Pro Tip

Present Perfect is your best friend for small talk: 'Have you tried…?' 'Have you been to…?' These open-ended questions keep conversations flowing naturally with visitors.

lessons.intro:
After seven lessons of building vocabulary, grammar, and confidence, it is time to put everything together in a realistic simulation. Module 1 has laid the foundations of your English skills: greeting visitors, introducing the APS, giving directions through the museum's 23 exhibition sections, talking about port history dating back to 1892, and making natural small talk. Every piece of the puzzle is now in place — what remains is to assemble them into a seamless, professional performance.

The Port of Santos Museum is a complex environment where guides must shift between hospitality, technical knowledge, and personal warmth — often within the same minute. A visitor might ask about the Lavoura locomotive from 1889, then need directions to the restroom, then want to know about Santos' best restaurants. The museum's 2,000+ artifacts, the port's record-breaking 179.8 million tonnes in 2024, and the building's heritage architecture from 1902 are all topics you may need to discuss at a moment's notice. This review lesson challenges you to combine all these skills naturally.

Think of this as a dress rehearsal before the real stage. The visitors are arriving, the museum doors are open, and you are ready to shine. In this lesson, you will simulate a complete first museum visit from welcome to goodbye, using everything you have learned in Lessons 1 through 7.
lessons.title: Review & Role Play: A First Visit
lessons.objective: Put it all together. Simulate a complete first museum visit from welcome to goodbye, using everything you've learned in Lessons 1-7. This is your first full dress rehearsal.

lessons.situation: A couple from New Zealand — Mark and Lisa — arrives at the museum for their first visit. They have no appointment, they are curious about everything, and they have exactly 45 minutes before their bus leaves for São Paulo. They want the full experience: a warm welcome, a quick overview of who runs the port, a walk through the main exhibits including the Lavoura locomotive, some interesting history, and a glimpse of the working port from the observation window. Give them the complete experience from the greeting to a warm farewell.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

This is your integration checkpoint. You've learned 7 grammar structures — now combine them fluidly in real conversation without thinking about rules.

Structure

To be: 'I am your guide.' | Regular verbs: 'The APS manages the port.'
May/Can: 'May I help you?' | Past Simple: 'The port opened in 1892.'
Imperatives: 'Please follow me.' | There is/are: 'There are 64 berths.'
Present Perfect: 'Have you visited Santos before?'

Port of Santos Examples

Good morning! I am your guide. Have you visited Santos before?
The port was inaugurated on February 2, 1892, and today it handles 179.8 million tonnes per year.
There are over 2,000 artifacts here. May I show you the Lavoura locomotive first?
Please follow me to the coffee section. The museum building dates back to 1902.

Watch Out!

Mixing tenses randomly: 'The port opens in 1892.' → 'The port opened in 1892.'
Over-thinking grammar during conversation → Focus on communication, not perfection.
Using only one structure → Mix all 7 naturally as the situation demands.

Pro Tip

Don't think about grammar during simulations — just communicate. If it flows naturally, you're doing it right. The goal is seamless conversation, not grammatical perfection.

lessons.intro:
Coffee did not just build the Port of Santos — it built modern Brazil. In the late 19th century, the lush plantations of the São Paulo hinterland produced more coffee than any other region on Earth. The dark, aromatic beans were the country's most valuable commodity, and every single sack had to travel down the Serra do Mar mountains, through the São Paulo Railway completed in 1867, and onto ships waiting at the Santos docks. The port and the coffee trade grew together, their fortunes inseparable.

The coffee barons — the "barões do café" — amassed extraordinary wealth and invested it back into Santos and São Paulo. They funded the construction of the magnificent Coffee Exchange Palace, a Beaux-Arts masterpiece where global coffee prices were determined until 1957. They built grand mansions, expanded the port's infrastructure, and attracted immigrants from Italy, Japan, Portugal, and beyond. At the peak of the coffee cycle, the commodity represented an astonishing 84% of Brazil's total exports. The Port of Santos Museum preserves this era through original coffee scales from the 1890s, shipping manifests, customs stamps, and photographs that capture the frenzy of the coffee boom.

In this lesson, you will tell the story of how coffee literally created the Port of Santos, using cause-and-effect connectors that make history feel logical and compelling. By the end, when a visitor asks "How did coffee make this port what it is today?", you will have an answer that captivates and informs.
lessons.title: The Coffee Cycle and the Birth of the Port
lessons.grammar: Past Simple — Cause and effect connectors
lessons.objective: Tell the story of how coffee built the Port of Santos using cause-and-effect language. You'll connect historical events like a pro storyteller.
lessons.situation: An Italian coffee importer named Signora Rossi is visiting the museum specifically because of the coffee connection. She runs a specialty coffee company in Milan and wants to understand exactly how coffee built the Port of Santos. She has already visited coffee plantations in Minas Gerais and now wants the port side of the story. She asks: 'How exactly did coffee make this port what it is today?' She expects historical detail, dates, and the personal stories behind the statistics.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Cause-and-effect connectors link reasons to results: because (reason), so (result), as a result, therefore, due to + noun. Combined with Past Simple for historical narration.

Structure

Because + clause: 'Because coffee exports grew, the port expanded.'
So + result: 'Coffee demand soared, so Santos became a major hub.'
Due to + noun: 'Due to the coffee boom, Santos transformed.'
As a result / Therefore: 'The railway arrived. As a result, exports tripled.'

Port of Santos Examples

Because coffee represented 84% of Brazil's exports, Santos became the wealthiest port in South America.
The São Paulo Railway opened in 1867, so coffee could reach the port faster.
Due to the coffee boom, the coffee barons built grand mansions and the Coffee Exchange Palace.
Millions of immigrants arrived in Santos. As a result, the city's population exploded.

Watch Out!

Because the boom, Santos grew. → Because of the boom, Santos grew. ('because of' + noun)
Santos grew, because so coffee was important. → Santos grew because coffee was important.
Due to coffee exports grew → Due to growing coffee exports (noun, not clause)

Pro Tip

Cause-and-effect makes history feel logical, not random — far more engaging than listing facts. 'Coffee prices soared in Europe, so the barons invested in a railway' tells a story visitors remember.

lessons.intro:
Museums are places where objects speak. A faded photograph, a rusted anchor, a yellowed shipping manifest — each artifact holds a story waiting to be told. The skill of describing exhibits in English goes beyond simple identification; it involves creating a narrative that connects the object to its historical moment and to the visitor's imagination. At the Port of Santos Museum, every display case contains a piece of maritime history that can spark curiosity, wonder, and conversation.

The museum's collection of over 2,000 artifacts spans the entire history of the port, from the colonial era to the present day. Among the highlights are the Lavoura locomotive from 1889, which carried coffee from plantations to the docks; the Lancha Igara, an early harbor patrol vessel; and the bow fragment of the Air Giorgis ship, one of the rarest artifacts in the collection. Scale models show the port as it looked in the 1920s, while original photographs capture dock workers, coffee sacks, and the grand warehouses of the coffee boom era. The museum opened on September 1, 1989, and its 23 exhibition sections have been expanded and curated continuously since then.

In this lesson, you will learn to describe these exhibits like a storyteller, using the Present Simple Passive voice to focus attention on the artifacts themselves. By the end, every object in the museum will become a conversation starter in your hands.
lessons.title: Describing Exhibits and Artifacts
lessons.objective: Describe museum exhibits like a storyteller. Learn to use the Passive Voice to talk about what was built, created, and preserved — so every artifact becomes a conversation starter.
lessons.situation: You are standing in front of a beautifully crafted scale model of the port from 1920, housed in a glass display case in the museum's main gallery. A curious visitor from the United Kingdom, an art history professor from Oxford, stops and asks: 'What exactly am I looking at? Who made this? What does it tell us about the port's history?' She is interested in both the artistic craftsmanship and the historical significance. Two other visitors nearby also lean in to listen.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

The Present Simple Passive (is/are + past participle) focuses attention on the OBJECT rather than the doer. Perfect for museum descriptions where the artifact matters more than who made it.

Structure

Active: 'The Port Authority preserves the collection.' → Passive: 'The collection is preserved by the Port Authority.'
Question: 'Is this artifact displayed permanently?'
Negative: 'This photograph is not dated.'

Port of Santos Examples

This scale model is made of wood and is considered one of the museum's finest pieces.
Over 2,000 artifacts are displayed across 23 exhibition sections.
The Lavoura locomotive from 1889 is preserved as a symbol of the coffee railway era.
The Lancha Igara is exhibited in the maritime section as an example of early harbor patrol vessels.

Watch Out!

This photo is take in 1905. → This photo was taken in 1905. (past = was/were)
The artifacts displayed chronologically. → The artifacts are displayed chronologically.
Overusing passive — mix with active for variety! → 'This model is made of wood. A local craftsman created it in 1925.'

Pro Tip

In museums, passive sounds natural and professional: 'This photograph was taken in 1905' beats 'Someone took this photograph in 1905.' Use it for elegant exhibit descriptions.

lessons.intro:
The waterfront of Santos is an open-air gallery of architectural history. Neoclassical facades from the coffee era stand alongside Art Deco warehouses from the 1930s and industrial structures that speak of modernization and progress. Many of these buildings are listed heritage sites, protected by Brazilian preservation laws. The museum building itself, designed by Engineer Weinschenck in 1902, is a stunning example of early 20th-century institutional architecture, with its imposing stone facade and elegant interior spaces.

For international visitors — especially architects, historians, and urban planners — the architectural diversity of the port area is often a highlight of the visit. The Coffee Exchange Palace, built in a lavish Beaux-Arts style, determined global coffee prices until 1957 and remains one of the most photographed buildings in Santos. The old customs houses, with their cast-iron columns imported from England in the 1890s, tell the story of a port that was once the richest in South America. Restoration projects have transformed several abandoned warehouses into vibrant cultural spaces, connecting the port's industrial past with its creative future.

In this lesson, you will describe the stunning architecture of the port area using properly ordered adjectives and compound descriptions. By the end, you will make buildings come alive with words — from "a beautiful restored neoclassical warehouse" to "a charming early-20th-century heritage building."
lessons.title: Architectural Heritage of the Port Area
lessons.objective: Describe the stunning architecture of the port area using properly ordered adjectives. From Art Deco warehouses to colonial customs houses — you'll make buildings come alive with words.
lessons.situation: An architecture student from the Politecnico di Milano is photographing the old warehouses and heritage buildings along the waterfront near the museum. She has a professional camera and a sketchbook, and she approaches you excitedly: 'These buildings are incredible! Can you tell me about the architectural styles here? Which ones are original and which have been restored? Who designed them?' She wants specific details about periods, materials, and architectural movements.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

English adjectives follow a fixed order: Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Color → Origin → Material → Purpose (mnemonic: OSASCOMP). Compound adjectives use hyphens: 'well-preserved,' '19th-century.'

Structure

Order: 'a [opinion] [age] [origin] [material] building'
Example: 'a beautiful old Brazilian stone warehouse'
Compound: 'a well-preserved Art Deco facade' / 'a 19th-century neoclassical customs house'

Port of Santos Examples

The museum occupies a beautiful early-20th-century Brazilian heritage building designed by Engineer Weinschenck in 1902.
The Coffee Exchange Palace is a stunning Beaux-Arts stone building that determined global coffee prices until 1957.
The waterfront features impressive restored neoclassical warehouses from the coffee boom era.
Santos has a charming vintage electric heritage tram that runs along the beachfront.

Watch Out!

A stone beautiful old warehouse → A beautiful old stone warehouse
A well preserved building → A well-preserved building (hyphen needed)
Piling up 5+ adjectives → Stick to 2-3 adjectives for natural flow

Pro Tip

Compound adjectives with hyphens pack rich detail efficiently: 'a well-preserved 1902 heritage building' gives visitors a vivid mental image in just a few words.

lessons.intro:
School groups bring a special energy to any museum — a mix of excitement, curiosity, and the occasional challenge of keeping young minds focused. When those students come from international schools or foreign countries, the language of engagement must shift to English. The Port of Santos Museum regularly welcomes groups of children and teenagers who arrive bubbling with energy and eager to explore the 23 exhibition sections and discover artifacts like the Lavoura locomotive from 1889.

Welcoming a group of twelve-year-olds requires a completely different approach than receiving a delegation of diplomats. Simpler vocabulary, interactive questions, fun facts, and a sense of adventure replace formal protocol and technical jargon. Did you know the port handled 179.8 million tonnes in 2024? That is enough cargo to fill millions of school buses! Did you know Santos used to be called the "port of death" because of yellow fever epidemics? Did you know the Lavoura locomotive is older than anyone's great-great-grandparents? These fun facts capture young imaginations in ways that statistics alone never could.

In this lesson, you will learn to engage and manage a group of excited students using "Let's" and "Shall we" for suggestions and inclusive language. By the end, you will be the kind of guide that students remember long after the field trip is over.
lessons.title: Welcoming a School Group
lessons.objective: Learn to engage and manage a group of excited students using suggestions and inclusive language. School groups are fun, energetic, and need a totally different approach.
lessons.situation: A group of 25 eighth-graders from an international school in São Paulo arrives at the museum with their teacher, Mrs. Patterson, and two parent chaperones. The students are speaking loudly in three different languages, taking selfies, and already poking at the display cases. Mrs. Patterson looks slightly stressed and asks you: 'Can you get their attention and give them the safety rules before we start? And please make it fun — they have been on a bus for two hours.' Welcome them and keep them engaged.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Let's + base verb makes casual inclusive suggestions. Shall we + base verb? is more polite. Both are perfect for guiding groups and keeping energy high.

Structure

Let's + base verb: 'Let's start with the coolest exhibit!'
Shall we + base verb? 'Shall we begin the tour?'
Negative: 'Let's not touch the artifacts.'
Response: 'Yes, let's!' / 'Good idea!'

Port of Santos Examples

Let's go see the Lavoura locomotive — it's from 1889!
Shall we start with a fun fact? The port used to be called the 'port of death' because of yellow fever epidemics!
Let's count how many ships we can see in the channel — can you guess how many pass through every day?
Shall we find out what 5.4 million TEUs looks like? That's a lot of containers!

Watch Out!

Let's us go. → Let's go! ('let's' already means 'let us')
Let's to start. → Let's start! (no 'to' after let's)
'Now look at the ships.' (command) → 'Let's check out the ships!' (inclusive)

Pro Tip

With kids, 'Let's' works much better than commands. 'Let's discover what's inside!' creates adventure. 'Shall we' adds a gentle, inviting tone for more formal school groups.

lessons.intro:
The Santos navigation channel is one of the most important waterways in the Southern Hemisphere. Stretching approximately 25 kilometers from the open Atlantic into the heart of the port complex, it is the artery through which billions of dollars in goods flow every year. The channel is maintained at approximately 15 meters depth through constant dredging operations, allowing massive Post-Panamax vessels to safely enter and exit the port. On a busy day, more than 30 vessels may transit this vital passage.

Harbor pilots — highly trained navigators with specialized knowledge of the channel's currents, tides, and underwater topography — board every incoming vessel at the channel entrance. They guide ships through the 25-kilometer journey to their designated berths among the port's 64 docking positions. Tugboats assist with maneuvering, especially for the largest vessels that can stretch over 300 meters in length. The port handled 179.8 million tonnes in 2024, and every single tonne passed through this channel. Understanding its geography and function is essential for answering the detailed questions that engineers, maritime professionals, and curious visitors frequently ask.

In this lesson, you will explain the navigation channel's geography and function using the Present Simple for processes and systems. By the end, you will turn engineering facts into fascinating stories that impress even the most technically knowledgeable visitors.
lessons.title: The Navigation Channel: Geography and Function
lessons.grammar: Present Simple for processes and systems
lessons.objective: Explain the navigation channel's geography and function using the Present Simple for processes. You'll turn engineering facts into fascinating stories for visitors.
lessons.situation: A maritime engineering student named Erik from the Norwegian University of Science and Technology is visiting the museum as part of his master's thesis research on South American port channels. He has a laptop open with diagrams and asks you detailed questions: 'How does the navigation channel work? How deep is it? How do ships get in and out safely? What is the dredging schedule?' He expects precise, technical answers with specific measurements and processes.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Use the Present Simple to describe how systems and processes work regularly. Sequence words (First, Then, Next, Finally) organize step-by-step explanations of permanent processes.

Structure

Process: 'Ships enter the channel. Pilots board. Tugboats guide them to berths.'
With sequence: 'First, the pilot boards. Then, tugboats connect. Finally, the ship docks.'
Frequency: 'Ships enter the channel daily / regularly / every hour.'

Port of Santos Examples

Ships enter the Santos navigation channel from the south and travel approximately 25 km to reach the inner port.
Harbor pilots board every incoming vessel to guide it through the 15-meter-deep channel.
The port handles over 179.8 million tonnes of cargo annually across its 64 berths.
On a busy day, more than 30 vessels transit the channel in both directions.

Watch Out!

Ships are entering the channel every day. → Ships enter the channel every day. (permanent process = simple)
The pilot is boarding for every ship. → The pilot boards every ship.
Forgetting -s: 'The channel extend 25 km.' → The channel extends 25 km.

Pro Tip

Sequence words help visitors follow complex processes: 'First, the pilot boards. Then, tugboats connect. Finally, the ship docks.' Clear, organized, professional.

lessons.intro:
No matter how well you prepare, visitors will always surprise you with unexpected questions. Some will be easy — "When was the museum built?" (1902, by Engineer Weinschenck). Some will be challenging — "What is the exact tonnage capacity of berth number 34?" And some will leave you momentarily speechless — "Was this port ever used for military purposes during World War II?" The mark of a true professional is not knowing every answer; it is knowing how to handle every question with grace and confidence.

At the Port of Santos Museum, visitors come armed with smartphones and prior research. A maritime history professor may ask about the economic impact of the 1929 coffee crisis on Santos. An engineering student might want to know the exact dimensions of the navigation channel. A journalist could press you on environmental controversies. The museum's 2,000+ artifacts across 23 sections, the port's 179.8 million tonnes of annual throughput, and 130 years of complex history mean that the potential questions are virtually unlimited. Whether you need to buy time, admit uncertainty, or redirect the conversation, there are specific English phrases that maintain your credibility.

In this lesson, you will build your toolkit for handling tricky questions using indirect and polite question forms. By the end, no question will catch you off guard — because you will have the language skills to respond professionally, even when you do not have the answer.
lessons.title: Handling Questions from Curious Visitors
lessons.objective: Handle tricky questions from curious visitors with confidence using indirect/polite question forms. No question will catch you off guard.

vocab.en: I'm not entirely sure, but I believe...

lessons.situation: A visitor studying World War II history asks you an unexpected question: 'Was this port ever used for military purposes during the Second World War? Were there submarine patrols in the channel?' You are not 100% sure about the military history details, but you know some general facts about the port during that era. Two other visitors are listening, waiting to hear your response. Handle the situation professionally without guessing or making up facts.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Indirect questions use a polite opening phrase followed by statement word order (no inversion). Start with: 'Could you tell me…,' 'Do you know…,' 'I was wondering…'

Structure

Direct: 'Where is the exit?' → Indirect: 'Could you tell me where the exit is?'
Direct: 'When was the museum built?' → Indirect: 'Do you happen to know when the museum was built?'
Direct: 'How long does the tour last?' → Indirect: 'I was wondering how long the tour lasts.'

Port of Santos Examples

Could you tell me how many artifacts the museum has? (Over 2,000!)
Do you happen to know when the port was inaugurated? (February 2, 1892.)
I was wondering how deep the navigation channel is. (Approximately 15 meters.)
May I ask which countries the port connects to? (Over 600 ports in 200 countries.)

Watch Out!

Can you tell me where is the exit? → Can you tell me where the exit is?
Could you tell me how does it work? → Could you tell me how it works?
Do you know when did it open? → Do you know when it opened?

Pro Tip

When you're unsure of an answer, buy time gracefully: 'That's a great question. Let me find out for you.' This maintains credibility while being honest.

lessons.intro:
True hospitality goes beyond information — it anticipates needs before they are expressed. A visitor who looks tired might appreciate the offer of a seat. A guest on a hot Santos summer day would welcome a glass of cold water. Someone with mobility challenges needs to know about elevator access before they encounter stairs. At the Port of Santos Museum, where the heritage building from 1902 spans multiple floors and the 23 exhibition sections require considerable walking, the comfort and well-being of every visitor is a priority.

The museum welcomes visitors of all ages and abilities. Elderly guests may need frequent rest breaks during the tour of the museum's 2,000+ artifacts. Families with small children need help finding baby-changing facilities. Visitors with disabilities require accessible routes and adapted services. On particularly warm days — Santos' tropical climate can reach 35°C — even young, healthy visitors appreciate a cool drink and a moment of shade. The ability to offer assistance proactively, using polite and considerate English, elevates your service from competent to truly exceptional.

In this lesson, you will learn to offer comfort and assistance using "Would you like...?" and "Would you prefer...?" By the end, you will make every visitor feel personally cared for — because the best hospitality is the kind that visitors feel without even having to ask.
lessons.title: Offering Assistance and Comfort
lessons.grammar: Would you like...? / Would you prefer...?
lessons.objective: Offer comfort and assistance to visitors using 'Would you like..?' and 'Would you prefer..?' Make every visitor feel personally cared for.
lessons.situation: Three situations arise simultaneously during a tour: An elderly Japanese gentleman in his 80s looks tired and is leaning against the wall after climbing stairs to the upper floor. A young mother from France is looking increasingly anxious because she cannot find the baby-changing room for her crying infant. And a visitor in a wheelchair is at the ground floor, unable to see the Lavoura locomotive exhibit on the upper level. You need to address all three with care, empathy, and practical solutions.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Would you like + noun/to-infinitive makes polite offers. Would you prefer + option A or option B gives choices. Both are essential for proactive hospitality.

Structure

Offer: 'Would you like + noun?' — 'Would you like some water?'
Offer: 'Would you like + to + verb?' — 'Would you like to sit down?'
Choice: 'Would you prefer + A or B?' — 'Would you prefer the elevator or the stairs?'

Port of Santos Examples

Would you like a glass of water? It's quite warm in the Santos summer.
Would you like to rest in the lounge before we continue to the second floor?
Would you prefer the extended tour with all 23 sections or the highlights tour?
Would you like me to arrange wheelchair access to the Lavoura locomotive exhibit?

Watch Out!

Would you like sit down? → Would you like to sit down?
Would you like a coffee or you prefer tea? → Would you prefer coffee or tea?
Do you want water? (too direct) → Would you like some water? (polite)

Pro Tip

Offering choices makes visitors feel valued and cared for: 'Would you prefer the elevator or the stairs?' is simple but powerful in a hospitality context.

lessons.intro:
Halfway through the course, it is time to step back and see how far you have come. In just fifteen lessons, you have built a foundation of vocabulary, grammar, and hospitality skills that would impress any international visitor to the Port of Santos Museum. From basic greetings and the verb "to be" to cause-and-effect narration, passive voice descriptions, and proactive comfort offers, your English toolkit has grown remarkably.

This review lesson brings together everything from Modules 1 and 2 in a comprehensive simulation. You will receive a group of travel bloggers from five different countries who are filming content about the museum, its 2,000+ artifacts, and the working port that handled 179.8 million tonnes in 2024. They will ask about the coffee cycle, the Lavoura locomotive from 1889, the architectural heritage of the 1902 building, and the view of the navigation channel from the observation deck. They will need directions, expect smooth transitions between topics, and judge every detail of your hospitality.

Consider this your mid-course milestone — a chance to celebrate progress and identify areas for the final push. You will guide these visitors through the museum's historical exhibits, handle their questions with confidence, and demonstrate that you are already well on your way to becoming a certified English-speaking port guide.
lessons.title: Review & Role Play: A Museum Tour (Part 1)
lessons.objective: Review and consolidate everything from Lessons 9-15 in a full museum tour simulation. Time for another dress rehearsal.
lessons.situation: A group of five travel bloggers and content creators from the United States, South Korea, Germany, Brazil, and Nigeria arrives for a full museum tour. They are filming everything for their channels and social media platforms, and they have high expectations for content. They want engaging storytelling, historical facts, technical details about port operations, and photogenic moments. They will be asking questions on camera and expect articulate, confident answers that their international audiences will find compelling.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Modules 1 and 2 covered 14 grammar structures. This review integrates: cause/effect, passive voice, adjective order, suggestions, processes, indirect questions, and offers — all in context.

Structure

Cause/effect: 'Because coffee boomed, the port expanded.'
Passive: 'This artifact is preserved by the museum.'
Adjectives: 'A beautiful restored neoclassical building.'
Suggestions: 'Let's start with the coffee section.'
Indirect Q: 'Could you tell me when this was built?'

Port of Santos Examples

This well-preserved 1902 building was designed by Engineer Weinschenck — shall we go inside?
Because coffee represented 84% of exports, the Coffee Exchange Palace was built to determine global prices.
Would you like to see the Lavoura locomotive? It is considered one of our most significant artifacts.
Could you tell me if you've visited any other port museums before?

Watch Out!

Using only one grammar structure throughout → Mix and match naturally
Over-thinking which grammar to use → Focus on communicating, not analyzing
Forgetting earlier structures → Review Module 1 basics regularly

Pro Tip

At this point, you have 15 grammar tools. The key is choosing the right one for each moment — that's what fluency looks like. Trust your instincts.

lessons.intro:
The opening moments of a guided tour set the tone for everything that follows. A strong, confident introduction tells visitors that they are in capable hands. It establishes expectations about the tour's duration, content, and conduct. It creates anticipation and excitement about what lies ahead. At the Port of Santos Museum, where visitors come from diverse backgrounds and with varying levels of English, a well-crafted opening is especially important for setting the stage.

Consider what a visitor needs to know in the first two minutes: who you are, how long the tour will take, what areas you will cover, and what rules apply. The museum's 23 exhibition sections and 2,000+ artifacts offer so much material that visitors appreciate knowing the itinerary upfront. Will you start with the coffee heritage gallery and its original scales from the 1890s? Will you visit the Lavoura locomotive from 1889? Will there be an observation deck stop where they can see the active port that handles 179.8 million tonnes annually? A clear preview builds anticipation and helps visitors engage more deeply with each exhibit.

In this lesson, you will learn to start a guided tour with impact using future forms — "will" for predictions and "going to" for planned activities. By the end, you will deliver opening remarks that hook your audience from the very first sentence and set them up for an unforgettable experience.
lessons.title: Starting a Guided Tour: Opening Remarks
lessons.objective: Learn to start a guided tour with impact. Use future forms to preview what's coming and get visitors excited about the experience ahead.

vocab.en: By the end of this tour...

lessons.situation: You are about to start a guided tour for a group of 15 international business executives from a multinational shipping company. They arrived by private bus from São Paulo and have exactly one hour before their next meeting. They expect a polished, professional opening that respects their time, previews the tour's content, and demonstrates that their guide is knowledgeable and well-prepared. The company's CEO is in the group and has visited several world-class port museums before.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Will expresses spontaneous decisions and predictions. Going to expresses planned actions and intentions. Both preview future events — ideal for tour openings.

Structure

Will + base: 'You will love this exhibit.' (prediction)
Going to + base: 'We are going to visit three areas.' (plan)
Question: 'Will we see the locomotive?' / 'Are we going to visit the observation deck?'

Port of Santos Examples

We're going to explore the museum's 23 exhibition sections over the next 45 minutes.
I think you'll be amazed by the Lavoura locomotive — it dates back to 1889.
First, we're going to visit the coffee heritage gallery, where you will see original documents from the 1890s.
By the end of this tour, you will understand why Santos handles 27% of Brazil's foreign trade.

Watch Out!

We will going to visit. → We are going to visit. OR We will visit.
'We'll see three areas' for a planned tour → 'We're going to see three areas' (planned)
I'm going to think you'll enjoy it. → I think you'll enjoy it. (prediction = will)

Pro Tip

Opening with future forms builds excitement: 'You're going to see some incredible artifacts today!' For tours, 'going to' (planned) usually sounds more professional than 'will' (casual/spontaneous).

lessons.intro:
The Port of Santos is a colossal machine of global trade. Every day, thousands of containers are loaded and unloaded, carrying everything from Brazilian soybeans and sugar to European electronics and Asian manufactured goods. In 2024 alone, the port processed 5.4 million TEUs of containerized cargo and handled a total of 179.8 million tonnes across all commodity types. The import-export process involves a complex chain of logistics, customs clearance, and documentation that keeps goods moving across oceans and international borders.

Brazil's position as an agricultural superpower means that the Port of Santos is the world's largest export point for soybeans, with tens of millions of tonnes shipped primarily to China each year. Sugar, coffee, citrus products, and frozen meat are also major export commodities. On the import side, fertilizers, chemicals, electronics, and vehicles arrive from Asia, Europe, and North America. The port connects to more than 600 ports in 200 countries, making it one of the most globally connected trade hubs on the planet. All of this activity passes through the port's 64 berths along 13 kilometers of quays.

In this lesson, you will explain import and export operations using the Present Simple Passive for processes. By the end, you will turn complex logistics into a clear, fascinating story that engages economists, business executives, and curious tourists alike.
lessons.title: Port Operations: Import and Export
lessons.objective: Explain import and export operations at the port using Present Simple Passive for processes. Turn logistics into a fascinating story.
lessons.situation: A logistics company executive from Singapore named Mr. Chen, who manages container operations across Southeast Asia, asks you during the tour: 'Walk me through how a container gets from a ship in the channel to a truck leaving the port. What are the steps? How long does it take? Who handles customs?' He knows the industry well and expects a detailed, step-by-step process explanation with realistic timelines and terminology that matches his professional experience.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Use is/are + past participle to describe processes where who performs the action is less important than what happens. Perfect for explaining port logistics step by step.

Structure

Process passive: 'Containers are loaded by gantry cranes.'
With agent: 'Cargo is inspected by customs officers.'
Without agent: 'Soybeans are exported in bulk.' (who does it is obvious)

Port of Santos Examples

Over 5.4 million TEUs are handled at Santos annually — making it the busiest container port in South America.
Soybeans are loaded onto bulk carriers at dedicated terminals and shipped primarily to China, the port's #1 trade partner.
All imported goods are inspected by customs before release — a process called customs clearance.
Sugar, coffee, and citrus are exported to over 600 ports across 200 countries.

Watch Out!

Containers are load onto ships. → Containers are loaded onto ships.
Cargo inspected by customs. → Cargo is inspected by customs.
Including 'by' when unnecessary: 'Coffee is exported by people.' → 'Coffee is exported worldwide.'

Pro Tip

For processes, passive voice sounds systematic and professional — exactly the tone you want when explaining port operations to logistics visitors.

lessons.intro:
One of the most magical moments in a guided tour is when the guide points to something happening in real time — a ship entering the 25-kilometer navigation channel, cranes swinging containers through the air, or a tugboat pushing a massive vessel into its berth at one of the port's 64 docking positions. The ability to describe what visitors can see right now, as it unfolds before their eyes, creates a vivid and immersive experience that no brochure or video can match.

From the observation points of the Port of Santos Museum, the living port becomes your most powerful visual aid. On any given day, visitors might see a Panamax container ship loaded with 10,000 TEUs gliding through the channel, bulk carriers being filled with soybeans bound for China, or harbor pilots boarding incoming vessels via pilot boats. The port handled 179.8 million tonnes in 2024, and that volume means there is always something happening — even at night, operations never stop. The waterfront stretches for 13 kilometers, offering panoramic views of cranes, ships, and the constant movement of trucks and trains.

In this lesson, you will bring the living, working port to life for visitors using the Present Continuous tense. By the end, you will narrate real-time scenes with energy and precision, turning a simple window view into the most memorable part of the tour.
lessons.title: Describing What Visitors Can See
lessons.objective: Describe what visitors can see from observation points using the Present Continuous — bringing the living, working port to life right before their eyes.

vocab.en: On your left you can see...
vocab.pt: À sua esquerda vocês podem ver...

lessons.situation: You are at the museum's observation deck with a group of eight visitors on a clear, sunny day. The port is buzzing with activity: a massive container ship is entering the channel guided by tugboats, gantry cranes are loading containers at a nearby terminal, a bulk carrier appears to be waiting at anchor, and trucks are visible on the access road carrying cargo. The visitors are pointing excitedly at different things. Describe everything they can see right now, as it happens.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Am/is/are + verb-ing describes actions happening RIGHT NOW. Perfect for live narration at observation points where visitors can see the working port in action.

Structure

Affirmative: 'A container ship is entering the channel.'
Negative: 'The cranes aren't working right now — it's a shift change.'
Question: 'Is that tugboat guiding the tanker?'

Port of Santos Examples

Look! A Panamax container ship is entering the navigation channel right now.
Over there, two tugboats are guiding a bulk carrier to berth number 12.
If you look to the left, gantry cranes are loading containers onto a vessel operated by Santos Brasil.
Right now, trucks are carrying soybeans from the hinterland — Santos exports more soybeans than any port in the world.

Watch Out!

The port is handling 27% of trade. → The port handles 27% of trade. (permanent fact = simple present)
Look, a ship enters! → Look, a ship is entering! (happening now = continuous)
I am knowing this fact. → I know this fact. (stative verbs don't use -ing)

Pro Tip

At observation points, present continuous brings the scene to life: 'Look over there — they're loading that tanker right now!' Pair with pointing for maximum impact.

lessons.intro:
Safety is non-negotiable in any port environment. The Port of Santos operates massive machinery capable of lifting 30-tonne containers, handles hazardous materials including chemicals and petroleum products, and maintains strict security protocols across its 64 berths and 13 kilometers of operational waterfront. When visitors enter areas near active operations, they must understand and follow safety rules — and it is your job to communicate those rules clearly in English.

The challenge is to be firm without being intimidating, authoritative without being cold. Visitors to the Port of Santos Museum come for enjoyment and education, not to feel like they are entering a military installation. Yet the port handled 179.8 million tonnes of cargo in 2024, and the machinery that moves that volume is powerful and potentially dangerous. Fire extinguishers, emergency exits, assembly points, restricted areas, and safety equipment are all part of the landscape. A well-delivered safety briefing protects visitors while keeping the tour atmosphere positive and professional.

In this lesson, you will deliver safety briefings and visitor rules using "must," "must not," and "have to." By the end, you will keep everyone safe while sounding friendly and professional — not bossy or intimidating.
lessons.title: Safety Briefings and Rules for Visitors
lessons.objective: Deliver safety briefings and visitor rules using 'must/must not/have to' — keeping everyone safe while sounding friendly, not bossy.
lessons.situation: Before starting a tour that includes areas near active port operations, you need to deliver a safety briefing to a mixed group of 20 visitors that includes elderly tourists, families with young children, a visitor in a wheelchair, and several energetic teenagers from an international school. The area includes operational zones where cranes operate, trucks move constantly, and noise levels are high. Make the briefing clear and important without sounding scary or dampening the group's enthusiasm.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Must = strong internal obligation/rule. Must not = prohibition (forbidden). Have to = external obligation. Key difference: 'don't have to' = not necessary (very different from 'must not'!).

Structure

Obligation: 'You must wear a safety vest.' / 'You have to stay behind the line.'
Prohibition: 'You must not touch the exhibits.'
Not necessary: 'You don't have to whisper — normal voices are fine.'

Port of Santos Examples

You must stay with the group at all times near the active berths.
Visitors must not cross the yellow safety line along the waterfront.
We have to wear hard hats in this section — it's a port safety regulation.
You don't have to rush — the tour of all 23 sections takes approximately 45 minutes.

Watch Out!

You mustn't to touch. → You mustn't touch the exhibits. (no 'to')
'You must not bring a camera' (= forbidden) vs. 'You don't have to bring a camera' (= optional) — BIG difference!
You must wear a vest! (too harsh) → 'Please remember that you must wear a safety vest in this area.' (firm but friendly)

Pro Tip

Soften rules with 'please' and 'for your safety': 'For your safety, please remember that you must stay behind the yellow line' sounds firm but caring.

lessons.intro:
The Port of Santos has undergone a remarkable technological transformation over its 130-year history. From manual laborers carrying 60-kilogram sacks of coffee on their backs in the 1890s to fully automated container terminals guided by artificial intelligence today, the story of port technology is a story of human ingenuity, adaptation, and relentless progress. Each era brought new machines, new methods, and new challenges that reshaped the waterfront and redefined what was possible.

The museum itself documents this evolution beautifully through its 2,000+ artifacts. The Lavoura locomotive from 1889 represents the steam-powered era that first connected the coffee plantations to the docks. Photographs from the early 1900s show dock workers using manual winches and pulleys. By the 1970s, container operations had arrived, revolutionizing how cargo was handled. Today, terminal operators like Santos Brasil, DP World, and BTP use automated gantry cranes, electronic tracking systems, and smart logistics platforms. The port's throughput grew from a few million tonnes in the early 20th century to a record 179.8 million tonnes in 2024 — a transformation made possible by relentless technological innovation.

In this lesson, you will describe how port technology has evolved using the Present Perfect with "since" and "for." By the end, you will tell the story of before-and-after transformations that fascinate technology journalists, engineers, and anyone curious about how the modern port came to be.
lessons.title: The Evolution of Port Technology
lessons.objective: Describe how port technology has evolved using Present Perfect with 'since' and 'for.' From manual cranes to automated terminals — what a journey.
lessons.situation: A technology journalist from Wired magazine is visiting the museum and asks you: 'How has this port changed over the years? What is different now compared to 50 years ago? What technology are you using today that did not exist back then?' She is preparing an article about smart ports in emerging markets and wants before-and-after stories that illustrate the dramatic transformation from manual operations to digital automation.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Present Perfect with since (point in time) and for (duration) describes changes from a specific point until now. Perfect for before/after comparisons of port technology.

Structure

Since + point: 'Technology has changed since the 1990s.'
For + duration: 'The port has used automated cranes for over 20 years.'
Question: 'How long has the port used this system?'

Port of Santos Examples

The port has grown from a few wooden wharves to 64 modern berths since its inauguration in 1892.
Container operations have transformed Santos for over 50 years, since the first container ships arrived in the 1970s.
Throughput has increased dramatically since 2010, reaching a record 179.8 million tonnes in 2024.
The museum has preserved port heritage for over 35 years, since it opened on September 1, 1989.

Watch Out!

The port has used cranes since 20 years. → …for 20 years. (duration = for)
Technology has changed for 1990. → …since 1990. (point in time = since)
The port changed since 2010. → The port has changed since 2010. (since = present perfect)

Pro Tip

Great for before/after comparisons: 'Since the 1970s, the port has grown from handling 5 million to 179.8 million tonnes.' Visitors love dramatic transformation stories.

lessons.intro:
A great guided tour is not just a series of stops — it is a flowing narrative with smooth transitions that keep visitors engaged from start to finish. Moving from the coffee era to modern operations, from indoor exhibits to outdoor observation points, from the Lavoura locomotive to a live view of automated cranes requires verbal bridges that connect ideas and maintain momentum. Awkward silences or abrupt topic changes can break the spell of even the most fascinating content.

The Port of Santos Museum's 23 exhibition sections cover an enormous range of topics — colonial history, the coffee boom, maritime technology, environmental initiatives, and modern logistics — all housed within the 1902 heritage building and its surrounding observation areas. As a guide, you might need to transition from describing a 130-year-old photograph to pointing out a container ship entering the channel in real time. You might shift from the solemn story of yellow fever epidemics to the exciting news of the port's record 179.8 million tonnes in 2024. Each transition is an opportunity to strengthen your narrative or lose your audience.

In this lesson, you will master the art of transition using linking words and discourse markers. By the end, your tours will flow like a well-written story — with no more awkward pauses between exhibit rooms or topic changes.
lessons.title: Transitioning Between Exhibits
lessons.objective: Master smooth transitions between exhibits using linking words and discourse markers. No more awkward pauses between rooms.

vocab.en: As we walk to the next area...

lessons.situation: You are in the middle of a guided tour and need to transition your group from the historical exhibits on the ground floor — where they have just been learning about the coffee era and colonial-era wooden wharves — to the modern operations section on the upper floor, where they will see displays about containerization, automation, and the port's record-breaking 2024 statistics. The mood needs to shift from nostalgic and reflective to exciting and forward-looking. Make the transition smooth and engaging.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Discourse markers guide your listener through your narrative: First / Next / Moving on / As I mentioned / Finally. They signal transitions, additions, and conclusions.

Structure

Topic change: 'Now, let's move to…' / 'Moving on to…'
Addition: 'Furthermore…' / 'In addition…' / 'What's more…'
Reference: 'As I mentioned earlier…' / 'Going back to…'
Conclusion: 'To sum up…' / 'That wraps up…'

Port of Santos Examples

That covers the history of coffee. Now, let's move on to the modern port operations section.
As I mentioned earlier, the port was inaugurated in 1892. In addition, this building dates back to 1902.
Speaking of which, the navigation channel we just discussed is visible from the observation deck — shall we take a look?
Before we move on, any questions about the Lavoura locomotive?

Watch Out!

'And then… and then… and then…' → Use varied markers: 'First… Next… Moving on… Finally…'
Overusing 'However' → Vary with 'Nevertheless,' 'On the other hand,' 'That said'
Abrupt topic changes with no transition → 'That brings us to our next area…'

Pro Tip

Good transitions show confidence and preparation. 'That covers the history section. Now, let's see how the port operates today!' keeps visitors engaged between exhibit rooms.

On that note, follow me to the final section of today's tour.
As you can see, every part of this port's story is connected.

lessons.intro:
Some visitors come to the Port of Santos Museum with deep technical knowledge and pointed questions about tonnage, draft, berth allocation, and maritime regulations. Engineers, naval architects, shipping executives, and port professionals expect precise, accurate answers delivered with confidence. When they ask "What is the maximum draft for vessels entering the Santos channel?" or "How many TEUs can your largest terminal handle per year?", vague answers will not satisfy them.

The Port of Santos provides impressive data for technical conversations. The navigation channel extends 25 kilometers at approximately 15 meters depth. The port's 64 berths handle vessels up to Post-Panamax class. Annual throughput reached 179.8 million tonnes and 5.4 million TEUs in 2024. Maritime safety is regulated by ANTAQ, Brazil's national waterway transportation agency. Terminal operators including Santos Brasil, DP World, and BTP maintain specific handling rates, turnaround times, and capacity figures that technical visitors expect guides to know.

In this lesson, you will answer technical questions about port operations using Passive Voice in both Present and Past tenses. By the end, you will sound like a real port expert — even when facing the toughest questions from shipping CEOs and maritime engineers.
lessons.title: Answering Technical Questions
lessons.objective: Answer technical questions about port operations using Passive Voice (Present and Past). Sound like a real expert, even with tough questions.
lessons.situation: The CEO of a major shipping company, accompanied by her chief engineer and operations director, asks detailed technical questions during a tour: 'What is the maximum draft for vessels entering your channel? How many ships can you handle per day? What is the average turnaround time at your container terminals? How does ANTAQ regulate operations here?' They expect precise, data-driven answers delivered with authority and confidence.
lessons.grammarDetail:
Quick Rule

Passive Voice has two main tenses: Present (is/are + past participle) for current facts, and Past (was/were + past participle) for historical events. Both are essential for technical descriptions.

Structure

Present: 'Over 4,000 ships are received annually.'
Past: 'The terminal was expanded in 2015.'
With agent: 'The channel is maintained by the Port Authority.'
Question: 'When was the last dredging operation completed?'

Port of Santos Examples

Over 179.8 million tonnes are handled annually across 64 berths.
The navigation channel was deepened to 15 meters to accommodate Post-Panamax vessels.
5.4 million TEUs were processed in 2024 — a new record for the port.
Maritime safety is regulated by ANTAQ, Brazil's national waterway transportation agency.

Watch Out!

The terminal was expand in 2015. → The terminal was expanded in 2015.
'is handled' for a past event → '179.8M tonnes were handled in 2024.' (past = was/were)
Mixing tenses: 'The channel is deepened last year.' → 'The channel was deepened last year.'

Pro Tip

Passive voice sounds authoritative when answering technical questions. 'This is managed by…' and 'That was constructed in…' = expert-level English.
